aliases: Telur Masin Hitam / Telur Sejuta Tahun

malay: Telur Masin Hitam / Telur Sejuta Tahun

region_of_origin: China (Hunan province origin); widely consumed in Singapore
food_subgroup: Preserved egg — alkaline-cured duck egg (century egg / pidan). 1 whole egg ~65g edible.

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn URI exists for century egg (pidan). Closest parent is preserved egg product. URI not assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Preserved eggs — duck egg, alkaline-cured (century egg / pidan). FoodEx2 matrix code A0CKP used as closest defensible anchor for preserved egg products.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for century egg. A0CKP (preserved egg products) used as closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for century egg. Not assigned to avoid fabrication.

main_ingredients: alkaline curing agents (sodium hydroxide, zinc oxide, salt, ash)

serving.natural_language_portion: One whole duck century egg, approximately 65g edible weight, typically served sliced as an accompaniment at zi char restaurants, congee stalls, or hawker centres.

nutrient_source.title: Duck Egg, whole, raw — USDA SR Legacy (NDB 01140) adapted for alkaline-cured century egg; cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (SR Legacy) + ICMR-NIN
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Duck egg SR Legacy values adapted for alkaline curing process. Sodium elevated significantly due to alkaline curing salts (692mg/100g vs ~146mg/100g raw). Protein and fat values consistent with preserved duck egg references. ICMR-NIN cross-referenced for micronutrient values.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Egg-Based Foods — University of Sydney GI Database; zero-carbohydrate foods assigned GI=0 by convention
gi_source.source_match_notes: Century egg contains 0g carbohydrate per 100g (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy duck egg reference adapted for alkaline curing). Foods with zero carbohydrate content are assigned GI=0 by established convention in the University of Sydney GI Database methodology. Glycemic load per 65g serving = (0 x 65) / 100 = 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content — 692mg/100g; approximately 450mg per whole egg (65g), representing ~23% of the HPB Singapore daily sodium limit of 2,000mg
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit century egg to half an egg per serving and avoid combining with other high-sodium foods such as soy sauce dressings, as a single zi char meal featuring century egg tofu can contribute 1,500–1,600mg sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI = 0 (zero carbohydrate, no glycaemic response); however, cholesterol content is significant at 347mg/100g (~225mg per whole egg), which warrants monitoring in T2DM patients with elevated LDL-C
type2_diabetes.guidance: Century egg is suitable for T2DM patients from a glycaemic perspective (GI=0), but those with elevated LDL-C should limit intake to one egg per day and monitor overall dietary cholesterol, as T2DM is associated with increased cardiovascular risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High cholesterol (347mg/100g) and high sodium (692mg/100g) both relevant to cardiovascular risk; saturated fat 3.8g/100g is moderate
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should restrict century egg to occasional consumption (no more than half an egg per serving), given the combined burden of dietary cholesterol (~225mg per egg) and sodium (~450mg per egg), both of which contribute to cardiovascular risk when consumed regularly.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beneficial selenium (25mcg/100g, ~16mcg per egg, ~30% RDA) and vitamin D (2mcg/100g) relevant to RA; high sodium is a secondary concern for RA patients on NSAIDs
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Century egg provides clinically relevant selenium and vitamin D for RA patients, but high sodium content warrants caution in those also managing hypertension or taking NSAIDs, which impair sodium excretion.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Century egg (皮蛋 / pidan) is a traditional Chinese preserved egg made from duck eggs cured in an alkaline mixture of sodium hydroxide, salt, ash, and zinc oxide for several weeks to months. The alkaline curing process transforms the egg white into a translucent, dark brown or black jelly-like solid and the yolk into a creamy, grey-green centre with a rich, complex umami flavour and a slightly sulphurous aroma. The texture is firm yet gelatinous, distinctly different from a cooked egg. Century egg originated in Hunan province, China, and is widely consumed across Singapore at congee stalls, zi char restaurants (most commonly as 皮蛋豆腐 — century egg with silken tofu), and as a cold platter ingredient during Chinese New Year. The primary clinical concern is sodium: 692mg per 100g, approximately 450mg per whole egg, representing 23% of the HPB Singapore daily sodium limit of 2,000mg. This is frequently underestimated because century egg is perceived as a condiment or side dish rather than a protein main. Cholesterol is also significant at 347mg/100g (~225mg per egg). The historical concern about lead contamination from traditional alkaline curing agents is not clinically relevant for modern Singapore-market century eggs, which use zinc oxide and comply with SFA and WHO food safety limits. Nutritionally, century egg provides 185kcal/100g, 12.3g protein, 12.3g fat, and zero carbohydrate, making it a GI=0 food suitable for glycaemic management. It is a source of selenium (25mcg/100g), vitamin D (2mcg/100g), vitamin B12 (1.6mcg/100g), and iron (2.5mg/100g).
meal_context: 皮蛋豆腐 (century egg with tofu)

confidence_reason: Nutrient values adapted from USDA SR Legacy duck egg reference (NDB 01140) with sodium elevated to reflect alkaline curing process, cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN. No direct HPB Singapore Foodo entry for century egg was identified. Adaptation is well-supported by the known chemistry of alkaline curing but is not an exact match to a primary Singapore-specific source.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero — century egg contains no carbohydrate by composition of the duck egg and the alkaline curing process introduces no carbohydrate. Sugar_g is naturally zero. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero (animal product). Vitamin_c_mg is naturally zero (egg product, no plant-derived vitamin C). Trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per source. The historical lead concern for century eggs is not clinically relevant for Singapore-market products regulated by SFA. Cholesterol value (347mg/100g) is clinically significant and should be flagged in dietary counselling. Per-serving sodium (450mg per 65g egg) is the primary clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management.
